title: Infinite Series
description: Module 2 — Convergence tests, absolute & conditional convergence

edges.label: a series \(\sum a_n\) is built by formally \_\_\_ all terms of \(\{a_n\}\)
edges.hint: The Σ symbol indicates this operation.

edges.label: truncating after \(n\) terms (only PART of the sum) gives \(s_n\), the \_\_\_ sum
edges.hint: Latin: "of a part". Adjective form: ___.

edges.label: \(\sum a_n\) converges \(\iff\) \(\{s_n\}\) \_\_\_
edges.hint: The series convergence is DEFINED by behavior of partial sums.

edges.label: if \(a_n \not\to 0\), then \(\sum a_n\) \_\_\_
edges.hint: Contrapositive: convergence forces \(a_n \to 0\).

edges.label: \(\sum a_n\) converges \(\iff\) \(\forall \varepsilon > 0\, \exists N\) such that \(|a_{m+1}+\cdots+a_n|\) \_\_\_
edges.hint: Cauchy condition for the partial-sum sequence.

edges.label: \(\sum ar^n\) converges precisely when \(|r|\) \_\_\_ \(1\)
edges.hint: Strict inequality matters here.

edges.label: \(\sum 1/n\) diverges, even though \(1/n\) \_\_\_ to \(0\)
edges.hint: \(a_n \to 0\) is necessary but NOT sufficient — harmonic is the textbook example.

edges.label: \(\sum 1/n^p\) converges \(\iff\) \(p\) \_\_\_ \(1\)
edges.hint: Harmonic (\(p=1\)) is the boundary case — diverges.

edges.label: for nonneg terms, \(\{s_n\}\) is automatically \_\_\_
edges.hint: Adding nonneg numbers can only make \(s_n\) go ↑.

edges.label: compare \(\sum f(n)\) with \(\int_1^\infty f(x)\,dx\) when \(f\) is positive and \_\_\_
edges.hint: Need monotone behavior to box the sum between two integrals.

edges.label: \(\int_1^\infty x^{-p}\,dx\) converges precisely when \(p\) \_\_\_ \(1\)
edges.hint: The improper integral is finite iff the exponent makes the antiderivative tend to a constant.

edges.label: if \(0 \le a_n \le b_n\) and \(\sum b_n\) converges, then \(\sum a_n\) \_\_\_
edges.hint: Smaller nonneg terms inherit the convergence.

edges.label: if \(\lim a_n/b_n = L \in (0, \infty)\), the two series share the \_\_\_ behavior
edges.hint: Both converge or both diverge.

edges.label: mimics geometric: if \(\lim |a_{n+1}/a_n| < 1\), then \(\sum a_n\) \_\_\_
edges.hint: The behavior is exactly that of \(\sum r^n\) with this ratio playing the role of \(r\).

edges.label: also mimics geometric: if \(\limsup |a_n|^{1/n} < 1\), then \(\sum a_n\) \_\_\_
edges.hint: Take \(n\)-th root instead of consecutive ratios.

edges.label: a \((-1)^n\) factor makes the signs \_\_\_ each term
edges.hint: \(+, -, +, -, \ldots\) — the signs ___ at each step.

edges.label: Leibniz: if \(b_n\) is decreasing AND \(b_n \to\) \_\_\_, the alternating series converges
edges.hint: The terms must shrink all the way to ___.

edges.label: requiring \(\sum |a_n| < \infty\) is a STRICTLY \_\_\_ condition than ordinary convergence
edges.hint: Implies ordinary convergence, but not the other way — so it is ___.

edges.label: if \(\sum |a_n|\) converges, then \(\sum a_n\) automatically \_\_\_
edges.hint: Absolute convergence is the stronger condition — the original series inherits convergence.

edges.label: a series that converges but \(\sum |a_n|\) does NOT — is called \_\_\_ convergent
edges.hint: The convergence depends on cancellation between positive and negative terms.

edges.label: classic case: \(\sum (-1)^n / n\) is \_\_\_ convergent
edges.hint: It converges (Leibniz) but \(\sum 1/n\) does not — so the convergence is ___.

edges.label: if \(\lim |a_{n+1}/a_n| = L < 1\), then \(\sum a_n\) converges \_\_\_
edges.hint: L < 1 gives the strongest form of convergence.

edges.label: if \(\limsup |a_n|^{1/n} = L\), the test is \_\_\_ when \(L = 1\)
edges.hint: The boundary case \(L=1\) gives no information either way.

edges.label: replacing \((-1)^n\) by any sequence with bounded \_\_\_ sums generalizes Leibniz
edges.hint: Dirichlet only needs \(\{B_n = \sum b_k\}\) to be bounded.
